revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] to [[Category:qq]][[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:qq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Redirect|Nose Myokenzan|sacred site near the summit|Nose Myokenzan (Nichiren Buddhism)|temple midway up|Hontaki-ji}} {{Infobox mountain | Name = Mount Myoken | Image = [[File:Mount Myoken (Nose)1.jpg|240px]] | Elevation_m = 660.1 | Range = [[Hokusetsu Mountain Range]] | Coordinates = {{Coord|34|55|44.328|N|135|27|59.245|E|display=inline}} | Location = {{ill|Kawanishi, Hyōgo|ja|兵庫県川西市}},
{{ill|Toyono District, Osaka|ja|大阪府豊能郡}} (Toyono Town and Nose Town) | Map = Japan Osaka Prefecture#Japan Hyogo Prefecture | Relief = 1 | Map_caption = Location of Mount Myoken in Osaka and Hyōgo | OSM_zoom = 10 }} '''Mount Myoken''' (妙見山) is a mountain straddling {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}} and {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, part of the [[Hokusetsu Mountain Range]]. == Overview == The mountain itself is a subject of worship. Near the summit is ''{{ill|Nose Myokenzan (Nichiren Buddhism)|ja|能勢妙見山 (日蓮宗)}}'', an important {{ill|Nichiren Buddhism|ja|日蓮宗}} temple in the Kansai region. This temple, officially named Murosan Shinnyo-ji Keigai Butsudō Nose Myokenzan, is an enclave of {{ill|Shinnyo-ji (Osaka Prefecture)|ja|真如寺}} in {{ill|Nose, Osaka|ja|能勢町}}, and attracts far more visitors than its parent temple. A fourth-class {{ill|Triangulation point|ja|三角点}} is located at the summit, within Toyono Town, Osaka. The triangulation marker is discreetly placed behind a memorial stone.{{Cite web|url=https://www.google.com/maps/@34.9288684,135.4673572,3a,90y,273.91h,30.3t/data=!3m8!1e1!3m6!1sAF1QipM6B_3w0CwvMR8Wu_YVqLdcIHiS5HpEXgePnAeg!2e10!3e11!6shttps:%2F%2Flh5.googleusercontent.com%2Fp%2FAF1QipM6B_3w0CwvMR8Wu_YVqLdcIHiS5HpEXgePnAeg%3Dw203-h100-k-no-pi-0-ya76.70306-ro-0-fo100!7i8704!8i4352?hl=ja |title=Google Street View: Mount Myoken Triangulation Point |access-date=2017-12-17 |date=2016-05 |publisher=[[Google Street View]] }}. Mount Myoken marks the southernmost natural habitat for {{ill|Japanese beech|ja|ブナ}} at altitudes of around 600 meters in Southwest Japan. Previously thought confined to Osaka Prefecture, eight beech trees have been identified in {{ill|Kawanishi, Hyōgo|ja|川西市}}, and a portion of the 9.5-hectare beech forest near the summit has been confirmed to extend into Hyōgo.http://www.pref.hyogo.lg.jp/JPN/apr/hyogoshizen/reddata2010/rdb2010/data/tan-itsu/tan-itsu_H25_10.pdfhttp://www.kobe-np.co.jp/news/hanshin/201503/0007829215.shtmlhttp://bunamori.org/ The beech habitats in Mount Myoken and {{ill|Izumikatsuragi Mountain|ja|和泉葛城山}} are designated as Natural Monuments. == History == === Summit === The summit was once home to Foraku-san Daikū-ji, reportedly founded by {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基}}. During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the {{ill|Nose clan|ja|能勢氏}}, descended from {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsunaka|ja|源満仲}}, began venerating {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}. In the early {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, Nissei, the 21st abbot of {{ill|Kuon-ji|ja|久遠寺}}, carved a new image of Myōken under the patronage of Lord Nose Yoritsugu and enshrined it in a temple built at Daikū-ji's former site, now known as Nose Myokenzan. The summit’s beech forest, over 10,000 years old, suggests that the site was a place of worship long before Daikū-ji's establishment. === Middle Slopes and Waterfalls === On the northern slope lies {{ill|Hontaki|ja|本滝}}, historically a site for ascetic practices since the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}. The falls are described in the {{ill|Settsu Meisho Zue|ja|摂津名所図会}} as “Myoken Falls,” frequented by Nose Myokenzan worshippers. Adjacent to the falls was Jōtomi-dō, later rebuilt as {{ill|Hontaki-ji|ja|本瀧寺}} in 1935 by Nichiren priest Noma Nisshō, becoming the head temple of {{ill|Myoken sect|ja|妙見宗}}. Many stone statues along the path to the falls reflect its past as a religious training ground. Other waterfalls include {{ill|Shintaki|ja|新滝}} (southwest slope) near {{ill|Shirataki Shrine|ja|白瀧神社}} and {{ill|Seiryū Falls|ja|清滝}} (east slope) near Myōryū-ji Temple. == Access == For detailed information on access to the summit (Nose Myokenzan Main Hall), see {{ill|Nose Myokenzan (Nichiren Buddhism)|ja|能勢妙見山 (日蓮宗)}}. * From {{ill|Ikeda Station (Osaka)|ja|池田駅}} (Hankyu Takarazuka Line), take the Hankyu Bus to Myōken-guchi (4 km hike). * From {{ill|Kameoka Station|ja|亀岡駅}} (JR Sagano Line), take the Keihan Kyoto Kotsu bus to Jinchi (5.4 km hike). * From {{ill|Myōken-guchi Station|ja|妙見口駅}} (Nose Electric Railway), hike 3.6 km via the Shintaki Trail. Five hiking routes are available.{{Cite web |url=https://noseden.hankyu.co.jp/guide/hiking/course.html |title=Hiking Routes for Mount Myoken |access-date=2023-11-29 |publisher=Nose Electric Railway }} * Cable cars and chairlifts ceased operation on December 3, 2023.{{cite pressrelease |url=https://noseden.hankyu.co.jp/upload_file/noseden/information/newsrelease202309221.pdf |title=End of Myoken Cable and Related Services |format=PDF |publisher=Nose Electric Railway |date=2023-09-22 |access-date=2023-11-01}}. == References == == See Also == * {{ill|List of mountains in Japan|ja|日本の山一覧}} * {{ill|Furusato Hyōgo 50 Mountains|ja|ふるさと兵庫50山}} == External Links == * [https://bunamori.org/ Nose Myokenzan Beech Conservation Society] * [http://www.myoken.org/ Official Website of Nose Myokenzan] * [http://www.hontakiji.com/ Hontaki-ji, Myoken-sect Head Temple] {{Commons|Category:original pages]][[Category:Myōkenzan (Osaka and Hyōgo)]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] revision.text:
{{Redirect|Nose Myokenzan|sacred site near the summit|Nose Myokenzan (Nichiren Buddhism)|temple midway up|Hontaki-ji}} {{Infobox mountain | Name = Mount Myoken | Image = [[File:Mount Myoken (Nose)1.jpg|240px]] | Elevation_m = 660.1 | Range = [[Hokusetsu Mountain Range]] | Coordinates = {{Coord|34|55|44.328|N|135|27|59.245|E|display=inline}} | Location = {{ill|Kawanishi, Hyōgo|ja|兵庫県川西市}},
{{ill|Toyono District, Osaka|ja|大阪府豊能郡}} (Toyono Town and Nose Town) | Map = Japan Osaka Prefecture#Japan Hyogo Prefecture | Relief = 1 | Map_caption = Location of Mount Myoken in Osaka and Hyōgo | OSM_zoom = 10 }} '''Mount Myoken''' (妙見山) is a mountain straddling {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}} and {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, part of the [[Hokusetsu Mountain Range]]. == Overview == The mountain itself is a subject of worship. Near the summit is ''{{ill|Nose Myokenzan (Nichiren Buddhism)|ja|能勢妙見山 (日蓮宗)}}'', an important {{ill|Nichiren Buddhism|ja|日蓮宗}} temple in the Kansai region. This temple, officially named Murosan Shinnyo-ji Keigai Butsudō Nose Myokenzan, is an enclave of {{ill|Shinnyo-ji (Osaka Prefecture)|ja|真如寺}} in {{ill|Nose, Osaka|ja|能勢町}}, and attracts far more visitors than its parent temple. A fourth-class {{ill|Triangulation point|ja|三角点}} is located at the summit, within Toyono Town, Osaka. The triangulation marker is discreetly placed behind a memorial stone.{{Cite web|url=https://www.google.com/maps/@34.9288684,135.4673572,3a,90y,273.91h,30.3t/data=!3m8!1e1!3m6!1sAF1QipM6B_3w0CwvMR8Wu_YVqLdcIHiS5HpEXgePnAeg!2e10!3e11!6shttps:%2F%2Flh5.googleusercontent.com%2Fp%2FAF1QipM6B_3w0CwvMR8Wu_YVqLdcIHiS5HpEXgePnAeg%3Dw203-h100-k-no-pi-0-ya76.70306-ro-0-fo100!7i8704!8i4352?hl=ja |title=Google Street View: Mount Myoken Triangulation Point |access-date=2017-12-17 |date=2016-05 |publisher=[[Google Street View]] }}. Mount Myoken marks the southernmost natural habitat for {{ill|Japanese beech|ja|ブナ}} at altitudes of around 600 meters in Southwest Japan. Previously thought confined to Osaka Prefecture, eight beech trees have been identified in {{ill|Kawanishi, Hyōgo|ja|川西市}}, and a portion of the 9.5-hectare beech forest near the summit has been confirmed to extend into Hyōgo.http://www.pref.hyogo.lg.jp/JPN/apr/hyogoshizen/reddata2010/rdb2010/data/tan-itsu/tan-itsu_H25_10.pdfhttp://www.kobe-np.co.jp/news/hanshin/201503/0007829215.shtmlhttp://bunamori.org/ The beech habitats in Mount Myoken and {{ill|Izumikatsuragi Mountain|ja|和泉葛城山}} are designated as Natural Monuments. == History == === Summit === The summit was once home to Foraku-san Daikū-ji, reportedly founded by {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基}}. During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the {{ill|Nose clan|ja|能勢氏}}, descended from {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsunaka|ja|源満仲}}, began venerating {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}. In the early {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, Nissei, the 21st abbot of {{ill|Kuon-ji|ja|久遠寺}}, carved a new image of Myōken under the patronage of Lord Nose Yoritsugu and enshrined it in a temple built at Daikū-ji's former site, now known as Nose Myokenzan. The summit’s beech forest, over 10,000 years old, suggests that the site was a place of worship long before Daikū-ji's establishment. === Middle Slopes and Waterfalls === On the northern slope lies {{ill|Hontaki|ja|本滝}}, historically a site for ascetic practices since the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}. The falls are described in the {{ill|Settsu Meisho Zue|ja|摂津名所図会}} as “Myoken Falls,” frequented by Nose Myokenzan worshippers. Adjacent to the falls was Jōtomi-dō, later rebuilt as {{ill|Hontaki-ji|ja|本瀧寺}} in 1935 by Nichiren priest Noma Nisshō, becoming the head temple of {{ill|Myoken sect|ja|妙見宗}}. Many stone statues along the path to the falls reflect its past as a religious training ground. Other waterfalls include {{ill|Shintaki|ja|新滝}} (southwest slope) near {{ill|Shirataki Shrine|ja|白瀧神社}} and {{ill|Seiryū Falls|ja|清滝}} (east slope) near Myōryū-ji Temple. == Access == For detailed information on access to the summit (Nose Myokenzan Main Hall), see {{ill|Nose Myokenzan (Nichiren Buddhism)|ja|能勢妙見山 (日蓮宗)}}. * From {{ill|Ikeda Station (Osaka)|ja|池田駅}} (Hankyu Takarazuka Line), take the Hankyu Bus to Myōken-guchi (4 km hike). * From {{ill|Kameoka Station|ja|亀岡駅}} (JR Sagano Line), take the Keihan Kyoto Kotsu bus to Jinchi (5.4 km hike). * From {{ill|Myōken-guchi Station|ja|妙見口駅}} (Nose Electric Railway), hike 3.6 km via the Shintaki Trail. Five hiking routes are available.{{Cite web |url=https://noseden.hankyu.co.jp/guide/hiking/course.html |title=Hiking Routes for Mount Myoken |access-date=2023-11-29 |publisher=Nose Electric Railway }} * Cable cars and chairlifts ceased operation on December 3, 2023.{{cite pressrelease |url=https://noseden.hankyu.co.jp/upload_file/noseden/information/newsrelease202309221.pdf |title=End of Myoken Cable and Related Services |format=PDF |publisher=Nose Electric Railway |date=2023-09-22 |access-date=2023-11-01}}. == References == == See Also == * {{ill|List of mountains in Japan|ja|日本の山一覧}} * {{ill|Furusato Hyōgo 50 Mountains|ja|ふるさと兵庫50山}} == External Links == * [https://bunamori.org/ Nose Myokenzan Beech Conservation Society] * [http://www.myoken.org/ Official Website of Nose Myokenzan] * [http://www.hontakiji.com/ Hontaki-ji, Myoken-sect Head Temple] {{Commons|Category:original pages]][[Category:Myōkenzan (Osaka and Hyōgo)]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] {{Redirect|Nose Myokenzan|sacred site near the summit|Nose Myokenzan (Nichiren Buddhism)|temple midway up|Hontaki-ji}} {{Infobox mountain | Name = Mount Myoken | Image = [[File:Mount Myoken (Nose)1.jpg|240px]] | Elevation_m = 660.1 | Range = [[Hokusetsu Mountain Range]] | Coordinates = {{Coord|34|55|44.328|N|135|27|59.245|E|display=inline}} | Location = {{ill|Kawanishi, Hyōgo|ja|兵庫県川西市}},
{{ill|Toyono District, Osaka|ja|大阪府豊能郡}} (Toyono Town and Nose Town) | Map = Japan Osaka Prefecture#Japan Hyogo Prefecture | Relief = 1 | Map_caption = Location of Mount Myoken in Osaka and Hyōgo | OSM_zoom = 10 }} '''Mount Myoken''' (妙見山) is a mountain straddling {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}} and {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, part of the [[Hokusetsu Mountain Range]]. == Overview == The mountain itself is a subject of worship. Near the summit is ''{{ill|Nose Myokenzan (Nichiren Buddhism)|ja|能勢妙見山 (日蓮宗)}}'', an important {{ill|Nichiren Buddhism|ja|日蓮宗}} temple in the Kansai region. This temple, officially named Murosan Shinnyo-ji Keigai Butsudō Nose Myokenzan, is an enclave of {{ill|Shinnyo-ji (Osaka Prefecture)|ja|真如寺}} in {{ill|Nose, Osaka|ja|能勢町}}, and attracts far more visitors than its parent temple. A fourth-class {{ill|Triangulation point|ja|三角点}} is located at the summit, within Toyono Town, Osaka. The triangulation marker is discreetly placed behind a memorial stone.{{Cite web|url=https://www.google.com/maps/@34.9288684,135.4673572,3a,90y,273.91h,30.3t/data=!3m8!1e1!3m6!1sAF1QipM6B_3w0CwvMR8Wu_YVqLdcIHiS5HpEXgePnAeg!2e10!3e11!6shttps:%2F%2Flh5.googleusercontent.com%2Fp%2FAF1QipM6B_3w0CwvMR8Wu_YVqLdcIHiS5HpEXgePnAeg%3Dw203-h100-k-no-pi-0-ya76.70306-ro-0-fo100!7i8704!8i4352?hl=ja |title=Google Street View: Mount Myoken Triangulation Point |access-date=2017-12-17 |date=2016-05 |publisher=[[Google Street View]] }}. Mount Myoken marks the southernmost natural habitat for {{ill|Japanese beech|ja|ブナ}} at altitudes of around 600 meters in Southwest Japan. Previously thought confined to Osaka Prefecture, eight beech trees have been identified in {{ill|Kawanishi, Hyōgo|ja|川西市}}, and a portion of the 9.5-hectare beech forest near the summit has been confirmed to extend into Hyōgo.http://www.pref.hyogo.lg.jp/JPN/apr/hyogoshizen/reddata2010/rdb2010/data/tan-itsu/tan-itsu_H25_10.pdfhttp://www.kobe-np.co.jp/news/hanshin/201503/0007829215.shtmlhttp://bunamori.org/ The beech habitats in Mount Myoken and {{ill|Izumikatsuragi Mountain|ja|和泉葛城山}} are designated as Natural Monuments. == History == === Summit === The summit was once home to Foraku-san Daikū-ji, reportedly founded by {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基}}. During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the {{ill|Nose clan|ja|能勢氏}}, descended from {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsunaka|ja|源満仲}}, began venerating {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}. In the early {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, Nissei, the 21st abbot of {{ill|Kuon-ji|ja|久遠寺}}, carved a new image of Myōken under the patronage of Lord Nose Yoritsugu and enshrined it in a temple built at Daikū-ji's former site, now known as Nose Myokenzan. The summit’s beech forest, over 10,000 years old, suggests that the site was a place of worship long before Daikū-ji's establishment. === Middle Slopes and Waterfalls === On the northern slope lies {{ill|Hontaki|ja|本滝}}, historically a site for ascetic practices since the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}. The falls are described in the {{ill|Settsu Meisho Zue|ja|摂津名所図会}} as “Myoken Falls,” frequented by Nose Myokenzan worshippers. Adjacent to the falls was Jōtomi-dō, later rebuilt as {{ill|Hontaki-ji|ja|本瀧寺}} in 1935 by Nichiren priest Noma Nisshō, becoming the head temple of {{ill|Myoken sect|ja|妙見宗}}. Many stone statues along the path to the falls reflect its past as a religious training ground. Other waterfalls include {{ill|Shintaki|ja|新滝}} (southwest slope) near {{ill|Shirataki Shrine|ja|白瀧神社}} and {{ill|Seiryū Falls|ja|清滝}} (east slope) near Myōryū-ji Temple. == Access == For detailed information on access to the summit (Nose Myokenzan Main Hall), see {{ill|Nose Myokenzan (Nichiren Buddhism)|ja|能勢妙見山 (日蓮宗)}}. * From {{ill|Ikeda Station (Osaka)|ja|池田駅}} (Hankyu Takarazuka Line), take the Hankyu Bus to Myōken-guchi (4 km hike). * From {{ill|Kameoka Station|ja|亀岡駅}} (JR Sagano Line), take the Keihan Kyoto Kotsu bus to Jinchi (5.4 km hike). * From {{ill|Myōken-guchi Station|ja|妙見口駅}} (Nose Electric Railway), hike 3.6 km via the Shintaki Trail. Five hiking routes are available.{{Cite web |url=https://noseden.hankyu.co.jp/guide/hiking/course.html |title=Hiking Routes for Mount Myoken |access-date=2023-11-29 |publisher=Nose Electric Railway }} * Cable cars and chairlifts ceased operation on December 3, 2023.{{cite pressrelease |url=https://noseden.hankyu.co.jp/upload_file/noseden/information/newsrelease202309221.pdf |title=End of Myoken Cable and Related Services |format=PDF |publisher=Nose Electric Railway |date=2023-09-22 |access-date=2023-11-01}}. == References == == See Also == * {{ill|List of mountains in Japan|ja|日本の山一覧}} * {{ill|Furusato Hyōgo 50 Mountains|ja|ふるさと兵庫50山}} == External Links == * [https://bunamori.org/ Nose Myokenzan Beech Conservation Society] * [http://www.myoken.org/ Official Website of Nose Myokenzan] * [http://www.hontakiji.com/ Hontaki-ji, Myoken-sect Head Temple] {{Commons|Category:original pages]][[Category:Myōkenzan (Osaka and Hyōgo)]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] Adjacent to the falls was Jōtomi-dō, later rebuilt as {{ill|Hontaki-ji|ja|本瀧寺}} in 1935 by Nichiren priest Noma Nisshō, becoming the head temple of {{ill|Myoken sect|ja|妙見宗}}. Many stone statues along the path to the falls reflect its past as a religious training ground. Other waterfalls include {{ill|Shintaki|ja|新滝}} (southwest slope) near {{ill|Shirataki Shrine|ja|白瀧神社}} and {{ill|Seiryū Falls|ja|清滝}} (east slope) near Myōryū-ji Temple. == Access == For detailed information on access to the summit (Nose Myokenzan Main Hall), see {{ill|Nose Myokenzan (Nichiren Buddhism)|ja|能勢妙見山 (日蓮宗)}}. * From {{ill|Ikeda Station (Osaka)|ja|池田駅}} (Hankyu Takarazuka Line), take the Hankyu Bus to Myōken-guchi (4 km hike). * From {{ill|Kameoka Station|ja|亀岡駅}} (JR Sagano Line), take the Keihan Kyoto Kotsu bus to Jinchi (5.4 km hike). * From {{ill|Myōken-guchi Station|ja|妙見口駅}} (Nose Electric Railway), hike 3.6 km via the Shintaki Trail. Five hiking routes are available.{{Cite web |url=https://noseden.hankyu.co.jp/guide/hiking/course.html |title=Hiking Routes for Mount Myoken |access-date=2023-11-29 |publisher=Nose Electric Railway }} * Cable cars and chairlifts ceased operation on December 3, 2023.{{cite pressrelease |url=https://noseden.hankyu.co.jp/upload_file/noseden/information/newsrelease202309221.pdf |title=End of Myoken Cable and Related Services |format=PDF |publisher=Nose Electric Railway |date=2023-09-22 |access-date=2023-11-01}}. == References == == See Also == * {{ill|List of mountains in Japan|ja|日本の山一覧}} * {{Redirect|Nose Myokenzan|sacred site near the summit|Nose Myokenzan (Nichiren Buddhism)|temple midway up|Hontaki-ji}} {{Infobox mountain | Name = Mount Myoken | Image = [[File:Mount Myoken (Nose)1.jpg|240px]] | Elevation_m = 660.1 | Range = [[Hokusetsu Mountain Range]] | Coordinates = {{Coord|34|55|44.328|N|135|27|59.245|E|display=inline}} | Location = {{ill|Kawanishi, Hyōgo|ja|兵庫県川西市}},
{{ill|Toyono District, Osaka|ja|大阪府豊能郡}} (Toyono Town and Nose Town) | Map = Japan Osaka Prefecture#Japan Hyogo Prefecture | Relief = 1 | Map_caption = Location of Mount Myoken in Osaka and Hyōgo | OSM_zoom = 10 }} '''Mount Myoken''' (妙見山) is a mountain straddling {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}} and {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, part of the [[Hokusetsu Mountain Range]]. == Overview == The mountain itself is a subject of worship. Near the summit is ''{{ill|Nose Myokenzan (Nichiren Buddhism)|ja|能勢妙見山 (日蓮宗)}}'', an important {{ill|Nichiren Buddhism|ja|日蓮宗}} temple in the Kansai region. This temple, officially named Murosan Shinnyo-ji Keigai Butsudō Nose Myokenzan, is an enclave of {{ill|Shinnyo-ji (Osaka Prefecture)|ja|真如寺}} in {{ill|Nose, Osaka|ja|能勢町}}, and attracts far more visitors than its parent temple. A fourth-class {{ill|Triangulation point|ja|三角点}} is located at the summit, within Toyono Town, Osaka. The triangulation marker is discreetly placed behind a memorial stone.{{Cite web|url=https://www.google.com/maps/@34.9288684,135.4673572,3a,90y,273.91h,30.3t/data=!3m8!1e1!3m6!1sAF1QipM6B_3w0CwvMR8Wu_YVqLdcIHiS5HpEXgePnAeg!2e10!3e11!6shttps:%2F%2Flh5.googleusercontent.com%2Fp%2FAF1QipM6B_3w0CwvMR8Wu_YVqLdcIHiS5HpEXgePnAeg%3Dw203-h100-k-no-pi-0-ya76.70306-ro-0-fo100!7i8704!8i4352?hl=ja |title=Google Street View: Mount Myoken Triangulation Point |access-date=2017-12-17 |date=2016-05 |publisher=[[Google Street View]] }}. Mount Myoken marks the southernmost natural habitat for {{ill|Japanese beech|ja|ブナ}} at altitudes of around 600 meters in Southwest Japan. Previously thought confined to Osaka Prefecture, eight beech trees have been identified in {{ill|Kawanishi, Hyōgo|ja|川西市}}, and a portion of the 9.5-hectare beech forest near the summit has been confirmed to extend into Hyōgo.http://www.pref.hyogo.lg.jp/JPN/apr/hyogoshizen/reddata2010/rdb2010/data/tan-itsu/tan-itsu_H25_10.pdfhttp://www.kobe-np.co.jp/news/hanshin/201503/0007829215.shtmlhttp://bunamori.org/ The beech habitats in Mount Myoken and {{ill|Izumikatsuragi Mountain|ja|和泉葛城山}} are designated as Natural Monuments. == History == === Summit === The summit was once home to Foraku-san Daikū-ji, reportedly founded by {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基}}. During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the {{ill|Nose clan|ja|能勢氏}}, descended from {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsunaka|ja|源満仲}}, began venerating {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}. In the early {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, Nissei, the 21st abbot of {{ill|Kuon-ji|ja|久遠寺}}, carved a new image of Myōken under the patronage of Lord Nose Yoritsugu and enshrined it in a temple built at Daikū-ji's former site, now known as Nose Myokenzan. The summit’s beech forest, over 10,000 years old, suggests that the site was a place of worship long before Daikū-ji's establishment. === Middle Slopes and Waterfalls === On the northern slope lies {{ill|Hontaki|ja|本滝}}, historically a site for ascetic practices since the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}. The falls are described in the {{ill|Settsu Meisho Zue|ja|摂津名所図会}} as “Myoken Falls,” frequented by Nose Myokenzan worshippers. Adjacent to the falls was Jōtomi-dō, later rebuilt as {{ill|Hontaki-ji|ja|本瀧寺}} in 1935 by Nichiren priest Noma Nisshō, becoming the head temple of {{ill|Myoken sect|ja|妙見宗}}. Many stone statues along the path to the falls reflect its past as a religious training ground. Other waterfalls include {{ill|Shintaki|ja|新滝}} (southwest slope) near {{ill|Shirataki Shrine|ja|白瀧神社}} and {{ill|Seiryū Falls|ja|清滝}} (east slope) near Myōryū-ji Temple. == Access == For detailed information on access to the summit (Nose Myokenzan Main Hall), see {{ill|Nose Myokenzan (Nichiren Buddhism)|ja|能勢妙見山 (日蓮宗)}}. * From {{ill|Ikeda Station (Osaka)|ja|池田駅}} (Hankyu Takarazuka Line), take the Hankyu Bus to Myōken-guchi (4 km hike). * From {{ill|Kameoka Station|ja|亀岡駅}} (JR Sagano Line), take the Keihan Kyoto Kotsu bus to Jinchi (5.4 km hike). * From {{ill|Myōken-guchi Station|ja|妙見口駅}} (Nose Electric Railway), hike 3.6 km via the Shintaki Trail. Five hiking routes are available.{{Cite web |url=https://noseden.hankyu.co.jp/guide/hiking/course.html |title=Hiking Routes for Mount Myoken |access-date=2023-11-29 |publisher=Nose Electric Railway }} * Cable cars and chairlifts ceased operation on December 3, 2023.{{cite pressrelease |url=https://noseden.hankyu.co.jp/upload_file/noseden/information/newsrelease202309221.pdf |title=End of Myoken Cable and Related Services |format=PDF |publisher=Nose Electric Railway |date=2023-09-22 |access-date=2023-11-01}}. == References == == See Also == * {{ill|List of mountains in Japan|ja|日本の山一覧}} * {{Redirect|Nose Myokenzan|sacred site near the summit|Nose Myokenzan (Nichiren Buddhism)|temple midway up|Hontaki-ji}} {{Infobox mountain | Name = Mount Myoken | Image = [[File:Mount Myoken (Nose)1.jpg|240px]] | Elevation_m = 660.1 | Range = [[Hokusetsu Mountain Range]] | Coordinates = {{Coord|34|55|44.328|N|135|27|59.245|E|display=inline}} | Location = {{ill|Kawanishi, Hyōgo|ja|兵庫県川西市}},
{{ill|Toyono District, Osaka|ja|大阪府豊能郡}} (Toyono Town and Nose Town) | Map = Japan Osaka Prefecture#Japan Hyogo Prefecture | Relief = 1 | Map_caption = Location of Mount Myoken in Osaka and Hyōgo | OSM_zoom = 10 }} '''Mount Myoken''' (妙見山) is a mountain straddling {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}} and {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, part of the [[Hokusetsu Mountain Range]]. == Overview == The mountain itself is a subject of worship. Near the summit is ''{{ill|Nose Myokenzan (Nichiren Buddhism)|ja|能勢妙見山 (日蓮宗)}}'', an important {{ill|Nichiren Buddhism|ja|日蓮宗}} temple in the Kansai region. This temple, officially named Murosan Shinnyo-ji Keigai Butsudō Nose Myokenzan, is an enclave of {{ill|Shinnyo-ji (Osaka Prefecture)|ja|真如寺}} in {{ill|Nose, Osaka|ja|能勢町}}, and attracts far more visitors than its parent temple. A fourth-class {{ill|Triangulation station|ja|三角点}} is located at the summit, within Toyono Town, Osaka. The triangulation marker is discreetly placed behind a memorial stone.{{Cite web|url=https://www.google.com/maps/@34.9288684,135.4673572,3a,90y,273.91h,30.3t/data=!3m8!1e1!3m6!1sAF1QipM6B_3w0CwvMR8Wu_YVqLdcIHiS5HpEXgePnAeg!2e10!3e11!6shttps:%2F%2Flh5.googleusercontent.com%2Fp%2FAF1QipM6B_3w0CwvMR8Wu_YVqLdcIHiS5HpEXgePnAeg%3Dw203-h100-k-no-pi-0-ya76.70306-ro-0-fo100!7i8704!8i4352?hl=ja |title=Google Street View: Mount Myoken Triangulation Point |access-date=2017-12-17 |date=2016-05 |publisher=[[Google Street View]] }}. Mount Myoken marks the southernmost natural habitat for {{ill|Japanese beech|ja|ブナ}} at altitudes of around 600 meters in Southwest Japan. Previously thought confined to Osaka Prefecture, eight beech trees have been identified in {{ill|Kawanishi, Hyōgo|ja|川西市}}, and a portion of the 9.5-hectare beech forest near the summit has been confirmed to extend into Hyōgo.http://www.pref.hyogo.lg.jp/JPN/apr/hyogoshizen/reddata2010/rdb2010/data/tan-itsu/tan-itsu_H25_10.pdfhttp://www.kobe-np.co.jp/news/hanshin/201503/0007829215.shtmlhttp://bunamori.org/ The beech habitats in Mount Myoken and {{ill|Izumikatsuragi Mountain|ja|和泉葛城山}} are designated as Natural Monuments. == History == === Summit === The summit was once home to Foraku-san Daikū-ji, reportedly founded by {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基}}. During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the {{ill|Nose clan|ja|能勢氏}}, descended from {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsunaka|ja|源満仲}}, began venerating {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}. In the early {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, Nissei, the 21st abbot of {{ill|Kuon-ji|ja|久遠寺}}, carved a new image of Myōken under the patronage of Lord Nose Yoritsugu and enshrined it in a temple built at Daikū-ji's former site, now known as Nose Myokenzan. The summit’s beech forest, over 10,000 years old, suggests that the site was a place of worship long before Daikū-ji's establishment. === Middle Slopes and Waterfalls === On the northern slope lies {{ill|Hontaki|ja|本滝}}, historically a site for ascetic practices since the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}. The falls are described in the {{ill|Settsu Meisho Zue|ja|摂津名所図会}} as “Myoken Falls,” frequented by Nose Myokenzan worshippers. Adjacent to the falls was Jōtomi-dō, later rebuilt as {{ill|Hontaki-ji|ja|本瀧寺}} in 1935 by Nichiren priest Noma Nisshō, becoming the head temple of {{ill|Myoken sect|ja|妙見宗}}. Many stone statues along the path to the falls reflect its past as a religious training ground. Other waterfalls include {{ill|Shintaki|ja|新滝}} (southwest slope) near {{ill|Shirataki Shrine|ja|白瀧神社}} and {{ill|Seiryū Falls|ja|清滝}} (east slope) near Myōryū-ji Temple. == Access == For detailed information on access to the summit (Nose Myokenzan Main Hall), see {{ill|Nose Myokenzan (Nichiren Buddhism)|ja|能勢妙見山 (日蓮宗)}}. * From {{ill|Ikeda Station (Osaka)|ja|池田駅}} (Hankyu Takarazuka Line), take the Hankyu Bus to Myōken-guchi (4 km hike). * From {{ill|Kameoka Station|ja|亀岡駅}} (JR Sagano Line), take the Keihan Kyoto Kotsu bus to Jinchi (5.4 km hike). * From {{ill|Myōken-guchi Station|ja|妙見口駅}} (Nose Electric Railway), hike 3.6 km via the Shintaki Trail. Five hiking routes are available.{{Cite web |url=https://noseden.hankyu.co.jp/guide/hiking/course.html |title=Hiking Routes for Mount Myoken |access-date=2023-11-29 |publisher=Nose Electric Railway }} * Cable cars and chairlifts ceased operation on December 3, 2023.{{cite pressrelease |url=https://noseden.hankyu.co.jp/upload_file/noseden/information/newsrelease202309221.pdf |title=End of Myoken Cable and Related Services |format=PDF |publisher=Nose Electric Railway |date=2023-09-22 |access-date=2023-11-01}}. == References == == See Also == * {{ill|List of mountains in Japan|ja|日本の山一覧}} * {{Redirect|Nose Myokenzan|sacred site near the summit|Nose Myokenzan (Nichiren Buddhism)|temple midway up|Hontaki-ji}} {{Infobox mountain | Name = Mount Myoken | Image = [[File:Mount Myoken (Nose)1.jpg|240px]] | Elevation_m = 660.1 | Range = [[Hokusetsu Mountain Range]] | Coordinates = {{Coord|34|55|44.328|N|135|27|59.245|E|display=inline}} | Location = {{ill|Kawanishi, Hyōgo|ja|兵庫県川西市}},
{{ill|Toyono District, Osaka|ja|大阪府豊能郡}} (Toyono Town and Nose Town) | Map = Japan Osaka Prefecture#Japan Hyogo Prefecture | Relief = 1 | Map_caption = Location of Mount Myoken in Osaka and Hyōgo | OSM_zoom = 10 }} '''Mount Myoken''' (妙見山) is a mountain straddling {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}} and {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, part of the [[Hokusetsu Mountain Range]]. == Overview == The mountain itself is a subject of worship. Near the summit is ''{{ill|Nose Myokenzan (Nichiren Buddhism)|ja|能勢妙見山 (日蓮宗)}}'', an important {{ill|Nichiren Buddhism|ja|日蓮宗}} temple in the Kansai region. This temple, officially named Murosan Shinnyo-ji Keigai Butsudō Nose Myokenzan, is an enclave of {{ill|Shinnyo-ji (Osaka Prefecture)|ja|真如寺}} in {{ill|Nose, Osaka|ja|能勢町}}, and attracts far more visitors than its parent temple. A fourth-class {{ill|Triangulation station|ja|三角点}} is located at the summit, within Toyono Town, Osaka. The triangulation marker is discreetly placed behind a memorial stone.{{Cite web|url=https://www.google.com/maps/@34.9288684,135.4673572,3a,90y,273.91h,30.3t/data=!3m8!1e1!3m6!1sAF1QipM6B_3w0CwvMR8Wu_YVqLdcIHiS5HpEXgePnAeg!2e10!3e11!6shttps:%2F%2Flh5.googleusercontent.com%2Fp%2FAF1QipM6B_3w0CwvMR8Wu_YVqLdcIHiS5HpEXgePnAeg%3Dw203-h100-k-no-pi-0-ya76.70306-ro-0-fo100!7i8704!8i4352?hl=ja |title=Google Street View: Mount Myoken Triangulation Point |access-date=2017-12-17 |date=2016-05 |publisher=[[Google Street View]] }}. Mount Myoken marks the southernmost natural habitat for {{ill|Japanese beech|ja|ブナ}} at altitudes of around 600 meters in Southwest Japan. Previously thought confined to Osaka Prefecture, eight beech trees have been identified in {{ill|Kawanishi, Hyōgo|ja|川西市}}, and a portion of the 9.5-hectare beech forest near the summit has been confirmed to extend into Hyōgo.http://www.pref.hyogo.lg.jp/JPN/apr/hyogoshizen/reddata2010/rdb2010/data/tan-itsu/tan-itsu_H25_10.pdfhttp://www.kobe-np.co.jp/news/hanshin/201503/0007829215.shtmlhttp://bunamori.org/ The beech habitats in Mount Myoken and {{ill|Izumikatsuragi Mountain|ja|和泉葛城山}} are designated as Natural Monuments. == History == === Summit === The summit was once home to Foraku-san Daikū-ji, reportedly founded by {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基}}. During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the {{ill|Nose clan|ja|能勢氏}}, descended from {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsunaka|ja|源満仲}}, began venerating {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}. In the early {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, Nissei, the 21st abbot of {{ill|Kuon-ji|ja|久遠寺}}, carved a new image of Myōken under the patronage of Lord Nose Yoritsugu and enshrined it in a temple built at Daikū-ji's former site, now known as Nose Myokenzan. The summit’s beech forest, over 10,000 years old, suggests that the site was a place of worship long before Daikū-ji's establishment. === Middle Slopes and Waterfalls === On the northern slope lies {{ill|Hontaki|ja|本滝}}, historically a site for ascetic practices since the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}. The falls are described in the {{ill|Settsu Meisho Zue|ja|摂津名所図会}} as “Myoken Falls,” frequented by Nose Myokenzan worshippers. Adjacent to the falls was Jōtomi-dō, later rebuilt as {{ill|Hontaki-ji|ja|本瀧寺}} in 1935 by Nichiren priest Noma Nisshō, becoming the head temple of {{ill|Myoken sect|ja|妙見宗}}. Many stone statues along the path to the falls reflect its past as a religious training ground. Other waterfalls include {{ill|Shintaki|ja|新滝}} (southwest slope) near {{ill|Shirataki Shrine|ja|白瀧神社}} and {{ill|Seiryū Falls|ja|清滝}} (east slope) near Myōryū-ji Temple. == Access == For detailed information on access to the summit (Nose Myokenzan Main Hall), see {{ill|Nose Myokenzan (Nichiren Buddhism)|ja|能勢妙見山 (日蓮宗)}}. * From {{ill|Ikeda Station (Osaka)|ja|池田駅}} (Hankyu Takarazuka Line), take the Hankyu Bus to Myōken-guchi (4 km hike). * From {{ill|Kameoka Station|ja|亀岡駅}} (JR Sagano Line), take the Keihan Kyoto Kotsu bus to Jinchi (5.4 km hike). * From {{ill|Myōken-guchi Station|ja|妙見口駅}} (Nose Electric Railway), hike 3.6 km via the Shintaki Trail. Five hiking routes are available.{{Cite web |url=https://noseden.hankyu.co.jp/guide/hiking/course.html |title=Hiking Routes for Mount Myoken |access-date=2023-11-29 |publisher=Nose Electric Railway }} * Cable cars and chairlifts ceased operation on December 3, 2023.{{cite pressrelease |url=https://noseden.hankyu.co.jp/upload_file/noseden/information/newsrelease202309221.pdf |title=End of Myoken Cable and Related Services |format=PDF |publisher=Nose Electric Railway |date=2023-09-22 |access-date=2023-11-01}}. == References == == See Also == * {{ill|List of mountains in Japan|ja|日本の山一覧}} * {{Redirect|Nose Myokenzan|sacred site near the summit|Nose Myokenzan (Nichiren Buddhism)|temple midway up|Hontaki-ji}} {{Infobox mountain | Name = Mount Myoken | Image = [[File:Mount Myoken (Nose)1.jpg|240px]] | Elevation_m = 660.1 | Range = [[Hokusetsu Mountain Range]] | Coordinates = {{Coord|34|55|44.328|N|135|27|59.245|E|display=inline}} | Location = {{ill|Kawanishi, Hyōgo|ja|兵庫県川西市}},
{{ill|Toyono District, Osaka|ja|大阪府豊能郡}} (Toyono Town and Nose Town) | Map = Japan Osaka Prefecture#Japan Hyogo Prefecture | Relief = 1 | Map_caption = Location of Mount Myoken in Osaka and Hyōgo | OSM_zoom = 10 }} '''Mount Myoken''' (妙見山) is a mountain straddling {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}} and {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, part of the [[Hokusetsu Mountain Range]]. == Overview == The mountain itself is a subject of worship. Near the summit is ''{{ill|Nose Myokenzan (Nichiren Buddhism)|ja|能勢妙見山 (日蓮宗)}}'', an important {{ill|Nichiren Buddhism|ja|日蓮宗}} temple in the Kansai region. This temple, officially named Murosan Shinnyo-ji Keigai Butsudō Nose Myokenzan, is an enclave of {{ill|Shinnyo-ji (Osaka Prefecture)|ja|真如寺}} in {{ill|Nose, Osaka|ja|能勢町}}, and attracts far more visitors than its parent temple. A fourth-class {{ill|Triangulation station|ja|三角点}} is located at the summit, within Toyono Town, Osaka. The triangulation marker is discreetly placed behind a memorial stone.{{Cite web|url=https://www.google.com/maps/@34.9288684,135.4673572,3a,90y,273.91h,30.3t/data=!3m8!1e1!3m6!1sAF1QipM6B_3w0CwvMR8Wu_YVqLdcIHiS5HpEXgePnAeg!2e10!3e11!6shttps:%2F%2Flh5.googleusercontent.com%2Fp%2FAF1QipM6B_3w0CwvMR8Wu_YVqLdcIHiS5HpEXgePnAeg%3Dw203-h100-k-no-pi-0-ya76.70306-ro-0-fo100!7i8704!8i4352?hl=ja |title=Google Street View: Mount Myoken Triangulation Point |access-date=2017-12-17 |date=2016-05 |publisher=[[Google Street View]] }}. Mount Myoken marks the southernmost natural habitat for {{ill|Japanese beech|ja|ブナ}} at altitudes of around 600 meters in Southwest Japan. Previously thought confined to Osaka Prefecture, eight beech trees have been identified in {{ill|Kawanishi, Hyōgo|ja|川西市}}, and a portion of the 9.5-hectare beech forest near the summit has been confirmed to extend into Hyōgo.http://www.pref.hyogo.lg.jp/JPN/apr/hyogoshizen/reddata2010/rdb2010/data/tan-itsu/tan-itsu_H25_10.pdfhttp://www.kobe-np.co.jp/news/hanshin/201503/0007829215.shtmlhttp://bunamori.org/ The beech habitats in Mount Myoken and {{ill|Izumikatsuragi Mountain|ja|和泉葛城山}} are designated as Natural Monuments. == History == === Summit === The summit was once home to Foraku-san Daikū-ji, reportedly founded by {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基}}. During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the {{ill|Nose clan|ja|能勢氏}}, descended from {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsunaka|ja|源満仲}}, began venerating {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}. In the early {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, Nissei, the 21st abbot of {{ill|Kuon-ji|ja|久遠寺}}, carved a new image of Myōken under the patronage of Lord Nose Yoritsugu and enshrined it in a temple built at Daikū-ji's former site, now known as Nose Myokenzan. The summit’s beech forest, over 10,000 years old, suggests that the site was a place of worship long before Daikū-ji's establishment. === Middle Slopes and Waterfalls === On the northern slope lies {{ill|Hontaki|ja|本滝}}, historically a site for ascetic practices since the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}. The falls are described in the {{ill|Settsu Meisho Zue|ja|摂津名所図会}} as “Myoken Falls,” frequented by Nose Myokenzan worshippers. Adjacent to the falls was Jōtomi-dō, later rebuilt as {{ill|Hontaki-ji|ja|本瀧寺}} in 1935 by Nichiren priest Noma Nisshō, becoming the head temple of {{ill|Myoken sect|ja|妙見宗}}. Many stone statues along the path to the falls reflect its past as a religious training ground. Other waterfalls include {{ill|Shintaki|ja|新滝}} (southwest slope) near {{ill|Shirataki Shrine|ja|白瀧神社}} and {{ill|Seiryū Falls|ja|清滝}} (east slope) near Myōryū-ji Temple. == Access == For detailed information on access to the summit (Nose Myokenzan Main Hall), see {{ill|Nose Myokenzan (Nichiren Buddhism)|ja|能勢妙見山 (日蓮宗)}}. * From {{ill|Ikeda Station (Osaka)|ja|池田駅}} (Hankyu Takarazuka Line), take the Hankyu Bus to Myōken-guchi (4 km hike). * From {{ill|Kameoka Station|ja|亀岡駅}} (JR Sagano Line), take the Keihan Kyoto Kotsu bus to Jinchi (5.4 km hike). * From {{ill|Myōken-guchi Station|ja|妙見口駅}} (Nose Electric Railway), hike 3.6 km via the Shintaki Trail. Five hiking routes are available.{{Cite web |url=https://noseden.hankyu.co.jp/guide/hiking/course.html |title=Hiking Routes for Mount Myoken |access-date=2023-11-29 |publisher=Nose Electric Railway }} * Cable cars and chairlifts ceased operation on December 3, 2023.{{cite pressrelease |url=https://noseden.hankyu.co.jp/upload_file/noseden/information/newsrelease202309221.pdf |title=End of Myoken Cable and Related Services |format=PDF |publisher=Nose Electric Railway |date=2023-09-22 |access-date=2023-11-01}}. == References == == See Also == * {{ill|List of mountains in Japan|ja|日本の山一覧}} * {{Redirect|Nose Myokenzan|sacred site near the summit|Nose Myokenzan (Nichiren Buddhism)|temple midway up|Hontaki-ji}} {{Infobox mountain | Name = Mount Myoken | Image = [[File:Mount Myoken (Nose)1.jpg|240px]] | Elevation_m = 660.1 | Range = [[Hokusetsu Mountain Range]] | Coordinates = {{Coord|34|55|44.328|N|135|27|59.245|E|display=inline}} | Location = {{ill|Kawanishi, Hyōgo|ja|兵庫県川西市}},
{{ill|Toyono District, Osaka|ja|大阪府豊能郡}} (Toyono Town and Nose Town) | Map = Japan Osaka Prefecture#Japan Hyogo Prefecture | Relief = 1 | Map_caption = Location of Mount Myoken in Osaka and Hyōgo | OSM_zoom = 10 }} '''Mount Myoken''' (妙見山) is a mountain straddling {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}} and {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, part of the [[Hokusetsu Mountain Range]]. == Overview == The mountain itself is a subject of worship. Near the summit is ''{{ill|Nose Myokenzan (Nichiren Buddhism)|ja|能勢妙見山 (日蓮宗)}}'', an important {{ill|Nichiren Buddhism|ja|日蓮宗}} temple in the Kansai region. This temple, officially named Murosan Shinnyo-ji Keigai Butsudō Nose Myokenzan, is an enclave of {{ill|Shinnyo-ji (Osaka Prefecture)|ja|真如寺}} in {{ill|Nose, Osaka|ja|能勢町}}, and attracts far more visitors than its parent temple. A fourth-class {{ill|Triangulation station|ja|三角点}} is located at the summit, within Toyono Town, Osaka. The triangulation marker is discreetly placed behind a memorial stone.{{Cite web|url=https://www.google.com/maps/@34.9288684,135.4673572,3a,90y,273.91h,30.3t/data=!3m8!1e1!3m6!1sAF1QipM6B_3w0CwvMR8Wu_YVqLdcIHiS5HpEXgePnAeg!2e10!3e11!6shttps:%2F%2Flh5.googleusercontent.com%2Fp%2FAF1QipM6B_3w0CwvMR8Wu_YVqLdcIHiS5HpEXgePnAeg%3Dw203-h100-k-no-pi-0-ya76.70306-ro-0-fo100!7i8704!8i4352?hl=ja |title=Google Street View: Mount Myoken Triangulation Point |access-date=2017-12-17 |date=2016-05 |publisher=[[Google Street View]] }}. Mount Myoken marks the southernmost natural habitat for {{ill|Japanese beech|ja|ブナ}} at altitudes of around 600 meters in Southwest Japan. Previously thought confined to Osaka Prefecture, eight beech trees have been identified in {{ill|Kawanishi, Hyōgo|ja|川西市}}, and a portion of the 9.5-hectare beech forest near the summit has been confirmed to extend into Hyōgo.http://www.pref.hyogo.lg.jp/JPN/apr/hyogoshizen/reddata2010/rdb2010/data/tan-itsu/tan-itsu_H25_10.pdfhttp://www.kobe-np.co.jp/news/hanshin/201503/0007829215.shtmlhttp://bunamori.org/ The beech habitats in Mount Myoken and {{ill|Izumikatsuragi Mountain|ja|和泉葛城山}} are designated as Natural Monuments. == History == === Summit === The summit was once home to Foraku-san Daikū-ji, reportedly founded by {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基}}. During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the {{ill|Nose clan|ja|能勢氏}}, descended from {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsunaka|ja|源満仲}}, began venerating {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}. In the early {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, Nissei, the 21st abbot of {{ill|Kuon-ji|ja|久遠寺}}, carved a new image of Myōken under the patronage of Lord Nose Yoritsugu and enshrined it in a temple built at Daikū-ji's former site, now known as Nose Myokenzan. The summit’s beech forest, over 10,000 years old, suggests that the site was a place of worship long before Daikū-ji's establishment. === Middle Slopes and Waterfalls === On the northern slope lies {{ill|Hontaki|ja|本滝}}, historically a site for ascetic practices since the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}. The falls are described in the {{ill|Settsu Meisho Zue|ja|摂津名所図会}} as “Myoken Falls,” frequented by Nose Myokenzan worshippers. Adjacent to the falls was Jōtomi-dō, later rebuilt as {{ill|Hontaki-ji|ja|本瀧寺}} in 1935 by Nichiren priest Noma Nisshō, becoming the head temple of {{ill|Myoken sect|ja|妙見宗}}. Many stone statues along the path to the falls reflect its past as a religious training ground. Other waterfalls include {{ill|Shintaki|ja|新滝}} (southwest slope) near {{ill|Shirataki Shrine|ja|白瀧神社}} and {{ill|Seiryū Falls|ja|清滝}} (east slope) near Myōryū-ji Temple. == Access == For detailed information on access to the summit (Nose Myokenzan Main Hall), see {{ill|Nose Myokenzan (Nichiren Buddhism)|ja|能勢妙見山 (日蓮宗)}}. * From {{ill|Ikeda Station (Osaka)|ja|池田駅}} (Hankyu Takarazuka Line), take the Hankyu Bus to Myōken-guchi (4 km hike). * From {{ill|Kameoka Station|ja|亀岡駅}} (JR Sagano Line), take the Keihan Kyoto Kotsu bus to Jinchi (5.4 km hike). * From {{ill|Myōken-guchi Station|ja|妙見口駅}} (Nose Electric Railway), hike 3.6 km via the Shintaki Trail. Five hiking routes are available.{{Cite web |url=https://noseden.hankyu.co.jp/guide/hiking/course.html |title=Hiking Routes for Mount Myoken |access-date=2023-11-29 |publisher=Nose Electric Railway }} * Cable cars and chairlifts ceased operation on December 3, 2023.{{cite pressrelease |url=https://noseden.hankyu.co.jp/upload_file/noseden/information/newsrelease202309221.pdf |title=End of Myoken Cable and Related Services |format=PDF |publisher=Nose Electric Railway |date=2023-09-22 |access-date=2023-11-01}}. == References == == See Also == * {{ill|List of mountains in Japan|ja|日本の山一覧}} * Adjacent to the falls was Jōtomi-dō, later rebuilt as {{ill|Hontaki-ji|ja|本瀧寺}} in 1935 by Nichiren priest Noma Nisshō, becoming the head temple of {{ill|Myoken sect|ja|妙見宗}}. Many stone statues along the path to the falls reflect its past as a religious training ground. Other waterfalls include {{ill|Shintaki|ja|新滝}} (southwest slope) near {{ill|Shirataki Shrine|ja|白瀧神社}} and {{ill|Seiryū Falls|ja|清滝}} (east slope) near Myōryū-ji Temple. == Access == For detailed information on access to the summit (Nose Myokenzan Main Hall), see {{ill|Nose Myokenzan (Nichiren Buddhism)|ja|能勢妙見山 (日蓮宗)}}. * From {{ill|Ikeda Station (Osaka)|ja|池田駅}} (Hankyu Takarazuka Line), take the Hankyu Bus to Myōken-guchi (4 km hike). * From {{ill|Kameoka Station|ja|亀岡駅}} (JR Sagano Line), take the Keihan Kyoto Kotsu bus to Jinchi (5.4 km hike). * From {{ill|Myōken-guchi Station|ja|妙見口駅}} (Nose Electric Railway), hike 3.6 km via the Shintaki Trail. Five hiking routes are available.{{Cite web |url=https://noseden.hankyu.co.jp/guide/hiking/course.html |title=Hiking Routes for Mount Myoken |access-date=2023-11-29 |publisher=Nose Electric Railway }} * Cable cars and chairlifts ceased operation on December 3, 2023.{{cite pressrelease |url=https://noseden.hankyu.co.jp/upload_file/noseden/information/newsrelease202309221.pdf |title=End of Myoken Cable and Related Services |format=PDF |publisher=Nose Electric Railway |date=2023-09-22 |access-date=2023-11-01}}. == References == == See Also == * {{ill|List of mountains in Japan|ja|日本の山一覧}} * {{Redirect|Nose Myokenzan|sacred site near the summit|Nose Myokenzan (Nichiren Buddhism)|temple midway up|Hontaki-ji}} {{Infobox mountain | Name = Mount Myoken | image = [[File:Mount Myoken (Nose)1.jpg|240px]] | Elevation_m = 660.1 | Range = [[Hokusetsu Mountain Range]] | Coordinates = {{Coord|34|55|44.328|N|135|27|59.245|E|display=inline}} | Location = {{ill|Kawanishi, Hyōgo|ja|兵庫県川西市}},
{{ill|Toyono District, Osaka|ja|大阪府豊能郡}} (Toyono Town and Nose Town) | Map = Japan Osaka Prefecture#Japan Hyogo Prefecture | Relief = 1 | Map_caption = Location of Mount Myoken in Osaka and Hyōgo | OSM_zoom = 10 }} '''Mount Myoken''' (妙見山) is a mountain straddling {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}} and {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, part of the [[Hokusetsu Mountain Range]]. == Overview == The mountain itself is a subject of worship. Near the summit is ''{{ill|Nose Myokenzan (Nichiren Buddhism)|ja|能勢妙見山 (Nichiren宗)}}'', an important {{ill|Nichiren Buddhism|ja|Nichiren宗}} temple in the Kansai region. This temple, officially named Murosan Shinnyo-ji Keigai Butsudō Nose Myokenzan, is an enclave of {{ill|Shinnyo-ji (Osaka Prefecture)|ja|真如寺}} in {{ill|Nose, Osaka|ja|能勢町}}, and attracts far more visitors than its parent temple. A fourth-class {{ill|Triangulation station|ja|三角点}} is located at the summit, within Toyono Town, Osaka. The triangulation marker is discreetly placed behind a memorial stone.{{Cite web|url=https://www.google.com/maps/@34.9288684,135.4673572,3a,90y,273.91h,30.3t/data=!3m8!1e1!3m6!1sAF1QipM6B_3w0CwvMR8Wu_YVqLdcIHiS5HpEXgePnAeg!2e10!3e11!6shttps:%2F%2Flh5.googleusercontent.com%2Fp%2FAF1QipM6B_3w0CwvMR8Wu_YVqLdcIHiS5HpEXgePnAeg%3Dw203-h100-k-no-pi-0-ya76.70306-ro-0-fo100!7i8704!8i4352?hl=ja |title=Google Street View: Mount Myoken Triangulation Point |access-date=2017-12-17 |date=2016-05 |publisher=[[Google Street View]] }}. Mount Myoken marks the southernmost natural habitat for {{ill|Japanese beech|ja|ブナ}} at altitudes of around 600 meters in Southwest Japan. Previously thought confined to Osaka Prefecture, eight beech trees have been identified in {{ill|Kawanishi, Hyōgo|ja|川西市}}, and a portion of the 9.5-hectare beech forest near the summit has been confirmed to extend into Hyōgo.http://www.pref.hyogo.lg.jp/JPN/apr/hyogoshizen/reddata2010/rdb2010/data/tan-itsu/tan-itsu_H25_10.pdfhttp://www.kobe-np.co.jp/news/hanshin/201503/0007829215.shtmlhttp://bunamori.org/ The beech habitats in Mount Myoken and {{ill|Izumikatsuragi Mountain|ja|和泉葛城山}} are designated as Natural Monuments. == History == === Summit === The summit was once home to Foraku-san Daikū-ji, reportedly founded by {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基}}. During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the {{ill|Nose clan|ja|能勢氏}}, descended from {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsunaka|ja|源満仲}}, began venerating {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}. In the early {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, Nissei, the 21st abbot of {{ill|Kuon-ji|ja|久遠寺}}, carved a new image of Myōken under the patronage of Lord Nose Yoritsugu and enshrined it in a temple built at Daikū-ji's former site, now known as Nose Myokenzan. The summit’s beech forest, over 10,000 years old, suggests that the site was a place of worship long before Daikū-ji's establishment. === Middle Slopes and Waterfalls === On the northern slope lies {{ill|Hontaki|ja|本滝}}, historically a site for ascetic practices since the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}. The falls are described in the {{ill|Settsu Meisho Zue|ja|摂津名所図会}} as “Myoken Falls,” frequented by Nose Myokenzan worshippers. Adjacent to the falls was Jōtomi-dō, later rebuilt as {{ill|Hontaki-ji|ja|本瀧寺}} in 1935 by Nichiren priest Noma Nisshō, becoming the head temple of {{ill|Myoken sect|ja|妙見宗}}. Many stone statues along the path to the falls reflect its past as a religious training ground. Other waterfalls include {{ill|Shintaki|ja|新滝}} (southwest slope) near {{ill|Shirataki Shrine|ja|白瀧神社}} and {{ill|Seiryū Falls|ja|清滝}} (east slope) near Myōryū-ji Temple. == Access == For detailed information on access to the summit (Nose Myokenzan Main Hall), see {{ill|Nose Myokenzan (Nichiren Buddhism)|ja|能勢妙見山 (Nichiren宗)}}. * From {{ill|Ikeda Station (Osaka)|ja|池田駅}} (Hankyu Takarazuka Line), take the Hankyu Bus to Myōken-guchi (4 km hike). * From {{ill|Kameoka Station|ja|亀岡駅}} (JR Sagano Line), take the Keihan Kyoto Kotsu bus to Jinchi (5.4 km hike). * From {{ill|Myōken-guchi Station|ja|妙見口駅}} (Nose Electric Railway), hike 3.6 km via the Shintaki Trail. Five hiking routes are available.{{Cite web |url=https://noseden.hankyu.co.jp/guide/hiking/course.html |title=Hiking Routes for Mount Myoken |access-date=2023-11-29 |publisher=Nose Electric Railway }} * Cable cars and chairlifts ceased operation on December 3, 2023.{{cite pressrelease |url=https://noseden.hankyu.co.jp/upload_file/noseden/information/newsrelease202309221.pdf |title=End of Myoken Cable and Related Services |format=PDF |publisher=Nose Electric Railway |date=2023-09-22 |access-date=2023-11-01}}. == References == == See Also == * {{ill|List of mountains in Japan|ja|日本の山一覧}} * {{Redirect|Nose Myokenzan|sacred site near the summit|Nose Myokenzan (Nichiren Buddhism)|temple midway up|Hontaki-ji}} {{Infobox mountain | Name = Mount Myoken | image = [[File:Mount Myoken (Nose)1.jpg|240px]] | Elevation_m = 660.1 | Range = [[Hokusetsu Mountain Range]] | Coordinates = {{Coord|34|55|44.328|N|135|27|59.245|E|display=inline}} | Location = {{ill|Kawanishi, Hyōgo|ja|兵庫県川西市}},
{{ill|Toyono District, Osaka|ja|大阪府豊能郡}} (Toyono Town and Nose Town) | Map = Japan Osaka Prefecture#Japan Hyogo Prefecture | Relief = 1 | Map_caption = Location of Mount Myoken in Osaka and Hyōgo | OSM_zoom = 10 }} '''Mount Myoken''' (妙見山) is a mountain straddling {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}} and {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, part of the [[Hokusetsu Mountain Range]]. == Overview == The mountain itself is a subject of worship. Near the summit is ''{{ill|Nose Myokenzan (Nichiren Buddhism)|ja|能勢妙見山 (Nichiren宗)}}'', an important {{ill|Nichiren Buddhism|ja|Nichiren宗}} temple in the Kansai region. This temple, officially named Murosan Shinnyo-ji Keigai Butsudō Nose Myokenzan, is an enclave of {{ill|Shinnyo-ji (Osaka Prefecture)|ja|真如寺}} in {{ill|Nose, Osaka|ja|能勢町}}, and attracts far more visitors than its parent temple. A fourth-class {{ill|Triangulation station|ja|三角点}} is located at the summit, within Toyono Town, Osaka. The triangulation marker is discreetly placed behind a memorial stone.{{Cite web|url=https://www.google.com/maps/@34.9288684,135.4673572,3a,90y,273.91h,30.3t/data=!3m8!1e1!3m6!1sAF1QipM6B_3w0CwvMR8Wu_YVqLdcIHiS5HpEXgePnAeg!2e10!3e11!6shttps:%2F%2Flh5.googleusercontent.com%2Fp%2FAF1QipM6B_3w0CwvMR8Wu_YVqLdcIHiS5HpEXgePnAeg%3Dw203-h100-k-no-pi-0-ya76.70306-ro-0-fo100!7i8704!8i4352?hl=ja |title=Google Street View: Mount Myoken Triangulation Point |access-date=2017-12-17 |date=2016-05 |publisher=[[Google Street View]] }}. Mount Myoken marks the southernmost natural habitat for {{ill|Japanese beech|ja|ブナ}} at altitudes of around 600 meters in Southwest Japan. Previously thought confined to Osaka Prefecture, eight beech trees have been identified in {{ill|Kawanishi, Hyōgo|ja|川西市}}, and a portion of the 9.5-hectare beech forest near the summit has been confirmed to extend into Hyōgo.http://www.pref.hyogo.lg.jp/JPN/apr/hyogoshizen/reddata2010/rdb2010/data/tan-itsu/tan-itsu_H25_10.pdfhttp://www.kobe-np.co.jp/news/hanshin/201503/0007829215.shtmlhttp://bunamori.org/ The beech habitats in Mount Myoken and {{ill|Izumikatsuragi Mountain|ja|和泉葛城山}} are designated as Natural Monuments. == History == === Summit === The summit was once home to Foraku-san Daikū-ji, reportedly founded by {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基}}. During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the {{ill|Nose clan|ja|能勢氏}}, descended from {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsunaka|ja|源満仲}}, began venerating {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}. In the early {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, Nissei, the 21st abbot of {{ill|Kuon-ji|ja|久遠寺}}, carved a new image of Myōken under the patronage of Lord Nose Yoritsugu and enshrined it in a temple built at Daikū-ji's former site, now known as Nose Myokenzan. The summit’s beech forest, over 10,000 years old, suggests that the site was a place of worship long before Daikū-ji's establishment. === Middle Slopes and Waterfalls === On the northern slope lies {{ill|Hontaki|ja|本滝}}, historically a site for ascetic practices since the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}. The falls are described in the {{ill|Settsu Meisho Zue|ja|摂津名所図会}} as “Myoken Falls,” frequented by Nose Myokenzan worshippers. Adjacent to the falls was Jōtomi-dō, later rebuilt as {{ill|Hontaki-ji|ja|本瀧寺}} in 1935 by Nichiren priest Noma Nisshō, becoming the head temple of {{ill|Myoken sect|ja|妙見宗}}. Many stone statues along the path to the falls reflect its past as a religious training ground. Other waterfalls include {{ill|Shintaki|ja|新滝}} (southwest slope) near {{ill|Shirataki Shrine|ja|白瀧神社}} and {{ill|Seiryū Falls|ja|清滝}} (east slope) near Myōryū-ji Temple. == Access == For detailed information on access to the summit (Nose Myokenzan Main Hall), see {{ill|Nose Myokenzan (Nichiren Buddhism)|ja|能勢妙見山 (Nichiren宗)}}. * From {{ill|Ikeda Station (Osaka)|ja|池田駅}} (Hankyu Takarazuka Line), take the Hankyu Bus to Myōken-guchi (4 km hike). * From {{ill|Kameoka Station|ja|亀岡駅}} (JR Sagano Line), take the Keihan Kyoto Kotsu bus to Jinchi (5.4 km hike). * From {{ill|Myōken-guchi Station|ja|妙見口駅}} (Nose Electric Railway), hike 3.6 km via the Shintaki Trail. Five hiking routes are available.{{Cite web |url=https://noseden.hankyu.co.jp/guide/hiking/course.html |title=Hiking Routes for Mount Myoken |access-date=2023-11-29 |publisher=Nose Electric Railway }} * Cable cars and chairlifts ceased operation on December 3, 2023.{{cite pressrelease |url=https://noseden.hankyu.co.jp/upload_file/noseden/information/newsrelease202309221.pdf |title=End of Myoken Cable and Related Services |format=PDF |publisher=Nose Electric Railway |date=2023-09-22 |access-date=2023-11-01}}. == References == == See Also == * {{ill|List of mountains in Japan|ja|日本の山一覧}} * {{Redirect|Nose Myokenzan|sacred site near the summit|Nose Myokenzan (Nichiren Buddhism)|temple midway up|Hontaki-ji}} {{Infobox mountain | Name = Mount Myoken | image = [[File:Mount Myoken (Nose)1.jpg|240px]] | Elevation_m = 660.1 | Range = [[Hokusetsu Mountain Range]] | Coordinates = {{Coord|34|55|44.328|N|135|27|59.245|E|display=inline}} | Location = {{ill|Kawanishi, Hyōgo|ja|兵庫県川西市}},
{{ill|Toyono District, Osaka|ja|大阪府豊能郡}} (Toyono Town and Nose Town) | Map = Japan Osaka Prefecture#Japan Hyogo Prefecture | Relief = 1 | Map_caption = Location of Mount Myoken in Osaka and Hyōgo | OSM_zoom = 10 }} '''Mount Myoken''' (妙見山) is a mountain straddling {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}} and {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, part of the [[Hokusetsu Mountain Range]]. == Overview == The mountain itself is a subject of worship. Near the summit is ''{{ill|Nose Myokenzan (Nichiren Buddhism)|ja|能勢妙見山 (Nichiren宗)}}'', an important {{ill|Nichiren Buddhism|ja|Nichiren宗}} temple in the Kansai region. This temple, officially named Murosan Shinnyo-ji Keigai Butsudō Nose Myokenzan, is an enclave of {{ill|Shinnyo-ji (Osaka Prefecture)|ja|真如寺}} in {{ill|Nose, Osaka|ja|能勢町}}, and attracts far more visitors than its parent temple. A fourth-class {{ill|Triangulation station|ja|三角点}} is located at the summit, within Toyono Town, Osaka. The triangulation marker is discreetly placed behind a memorial stone.{{Cite web|url=https://www.google.com/maps/@34.9288684,135.4673572,3a,90y,273.91h,30.3t/data=!3m8!1e1!3m6!1sAF1QipM6B_3w0CwvMR8Wu_YVqLdcIHiS5HpEXgePnAeg!2e10!3e11!6shttps:%2F%2Flh5.googleusercontent.com%2Fp%2FAF1QipM6B_3w0CwvMR8Wu_YVqLdcIHiS5HpEXgePnAeg%3Dw203-h100-k-no-pi-0-ya76.70306-ro-0-fo100!7i8704!8i4352?hl=ja |title=Google Street View: Mount Myoken Triangulation Point |access-date=2017-12-17 |date=2016-05 |publisher=[[Google Street View]] }}. Mount Myoken marks the southernmost natural habitat for {{ill|Japanese beech|ja|ブナ}} at altitudes of around 600 meters in Southwest Japan. Previously thought confined to Osaka Prefecture, eight beech trees have been identified in {{ill|Kawanishi, Hyōgo|ja|川西市}}, and a portion of the 9.5-hectare beech forest near the summit has been confirmed to extend into Hyōgo.http://www.pref.hyogo.lg.jp/JPN/apr/hyogoshizen/reddata2010/rdb2010/data/tan-itsu/tan-itsu_H25_10.pdfhttp://www.kobe-np.co.jp/news/hanshin/201503/0007829215.shtmlhttp://bunamori.org/ The beech habitats in Mount Myoken and {{ill|Izumikatsuragi Mountain|ja|和泉葛城山}} are designated as Natural Monuments. == History == === Summit === The summit was once home to Foraku-san Daikū-ji, reportedly founded by {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基}}. During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the {{ill|Nose clan|ja|能勢氏}}, descended from {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsunaka|ja|源満仲}}, began venerating {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}. In the early {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, Nissei, the 21st abbot of {{ill|Kuon-ji|ja|久遠寺}}, carved a new image of Myōken under the patronage of Lord Nose Yoritsugu and enshrined it in a temple built at Daikū-ji's former site, now known as Nose Myokenzan. The summit’s beech forest, over 10,000 years old, suggests that the site was a place of worship long before Daikū-ji's establishment. === Middle Slopes and Waterfalls === On the northern slope lies {{ill|Hontaki|ja|本滝}}, historically a site for ascetic practices since the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}. The falls are described in the {{ill|Settsu Meisho Zue|ja|摂津名所図会}} as “Myoken Falls,” frequented by Nose Myokenzan worshippers. Adjacent to the falls was Jōtomi-dō, later rebuilt as {{ill|Hontaki-ji|ja|本瀧寺}} in 1935 by Nichiren priest Noma Nisshō, becoming the head temple of {{ill|Myoken sect|ja|妙見宗}}. Many stone statues along the path to the falls reflect its past as a religious training ground. Other waterfalls include {{ill|Shintaki|ja|新滝}} (southwest slope) near {{ill|Shirataki Shrine|ja|白瀧神社}} and {{ill|Seiryū Falls|ja|清滝}} (east slope) near Myōryū-ji Temple. == Access == For detailed information on access to the summit (Nose Myokenzan Main Hall), see {{ill|Nose Myokenzan (Nichiren Buddhism)|ja|能勢妙見山 (Nichiren宗)}}. * From {{ill|Ikeda Station (Osaka)|ja|池田駅}} (Hankyu Takarazuka Line), take the Hankyu Bus to Myōken-guchi (4 km hike). * From {{ill|Kameoka Station|ja|亀岡駅}} (JR Sagano Line), take the Keihan Kyoto Kotsu bus to Jinchi (5.4 km hike). * From {{ill|Myōken-guchi Station|ja|妙見口駅}} (Nose Electric Railway), hike 3.6 km via the Shintaki Trail. Five hiking routes are available.{{Cite web |url=https://noseden.hankyu.co.jp/guide/hiking/course.html |title=Hiking Routes for Mount Myoken |access-date=2023-11-29 |publisher=Nose Electric Railway }} * Cable cars and chairlifts ceased operation on December 3, 2023.{{cite pressrelease |url=https://noseden.hankyu.co.jp/upload_file/noseden/information/newsrelease202309221.pdf |title=End of Myoken Cable and Related Services |format=PDF |publisher=Nose Electric Railway |date=2023-09-22 |access-date=2023-11-01}}. == References == == See Also == * {{ill|List of mountains in Japan|ja|日本の山一覧}} * {{Redirect|Nose Myokenzan|sacred site near the summit|Nose Myokenzan (Nichiren Buddhism)|temple midway up|Hontaki-ji}} {{Infobox mountain | Name = Mount Myoken | image = [[File:Mount Myoken (Nose)1.jpg|240px]] | Elevation_m = 660.1 | Range = [[Hokusetsu Mountain Range]] | Coordinates = {{Coord|34|55|44.328|N|135|27|59.245|E|display=inline}} | Location = {{ill|Kawanishi, Hyōgo|ja|兵庫県川西市}},
{{ill|Toyono District, Osaka|ja|大阪府豊能郡}} (Toyono Town and Nose Town) | Map = Japan Osaka Prefecture#Japan Hyogo Prefecture | Relief = 1 | Map_caption = Location of Mount Myoken in Osaka and Hyōgo | OSM_zoom = 10 }} '''Mount Myoken''' (妙見山) is a mountain straddling {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}} and {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, part of the [[Hokusetsu Mountain Range]]. == Overview == The mountain itself is a subject of worship. Near the summit is ''{{ill|Nose Myokenzan (Nichiren Buddhism)|ja|能勢妙見山 (Nichiren宗)}}'', an important {{ill|Nichiren Buddhism|ja|Nichiren宗}} temple in the Kansai region. This temple, officially named Murosan Shinnyo-ji Keigai Butsudō Nose Myokenzan, is an enclave of {{ill|Shinnyo-ji (Osaka Prefecture)|ja|真如寺}} in {{ill|Nose, Osaka|ja|能勢町}}, and attracts far more visitors than its parent temple. A fourth-class {{ill|Triangulation station|ja|三角点}} is located at the summit, within Toyono Town, Osaka. The triangulation marker is discreetly placed behind a memorial stone.{{Cite web|url=https://www.google.com/maps/@34.9288684,135.4673572,3a,90y,273.91h,30.3t/data=!3m8!1e1!3m6!1sAF1QipM6B_3w0CwvMR8Wu_YVqLdcIHiS5HpEXgePnAeg!2e10!3e11!6shttps:%2F%2Flh5.googleusercontent.com%2Fp%2FAF1QipM6B_3w0CwvMR8Wu_YVqLdcIHiS5HpEXgePnAeg%3Dw203-h100-k-no-pi-0-ya76.70306-ro-0-fo100!7i8704!8i4352?hl=ja |title=Google Street View: Mount Myoken Triangulation Point |access-date=2017-12-17 |date=2016-05 |publisher=[[Google Street View]] }}. Mount Myoken marks the southernmost natural habitat for {{ill|Japanese beech|ja|ブナ}} at altitudes of around 600 meters in Southwest Japan. Previously thought confined to Osaka Prefecture, eight beech trees have been identified in {{ill|Kawanishi, Hyōgo|ja|川西市}}, and a portion of the 9.5-hectare beech forest near the summit has been confirmed to extend into Hyōgo.http://www.pref.hyogo.lg.jp/JPN/apr/hyogoshizen/reddata2010/rdb2010/data/tan-itsu/tan-itsu_H25_10.pdfhttp://www.kobe-np.co.jp/news/hanshin/201503/0007829215.shtmlhttp://bunamori.org/ The beech habitats in Mount Myoken and {{ill|Izumikatsuragi Mountain|ja|和泉葛城山}} are designated as Natural Monuments. == History == === Summit === The summit was once home to Foraku-san Daikū-ji, reportedly founded by {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基}}. During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the {{ill|Nose clan|ja|能勢氏}}, descended from {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsunaka|ja|源満仲}}, began venerating {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}. In the early {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, Nissei, the 21st abbot of {{ill|Kuon-ji|ja|久遠寺}}, carved a new image of Myōken under the patronage of Lord Nose Yoritsugu and enshrined it in a temple built at Daikū-ji's former site, now known as Nose Myokenzan. The summit’s beech forest, over 10,000 years old, suggests that the site was a place of worship long before Daikū-ji's establishment. === Middle Slopes and Waterfalls === On the northern slope lies {{ill|Hontaki|ja|本滝}}, historically a site for ascetic practices since the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}. The falls are described in the {{ill|Settsu Meisho Zue|ja|摂津名所図会}} as “Myoken Falls,” frequented by Nose Myokenzan worshippers. Adjacent to the falls was Jōtomi-dō, later rebuilt as {{ill|Hontaki-ji|ja|本瀧寺}} in 1935 by Nichiren priest Noma Nisshō, becoming the head temple of {{ill|Myoken sect|ja|妙見宗}}. Many stone statues along the path to the falls reflect its past as a religious training ground. Other waterfalls include {{ill|Shintaki|ja|新滝}} (southwest slope) near {{ill|Shirataki Shrine|ja|白瀧神社}} and {{ill|Seiryū Falls|ja|清滝}} (east slope) near Myōryū-ji Temple. == Access == For detailed information on access to the summit (Nose Myokenzan Main Hall), see {{ill|Nose Myokenzan (Nichiren Buddhism)|ja|能勢妙見山 (Nichiren宗)}}. * From {{ill|Ikeda Station (Osaka)|ja|池田駅}} (Hankyu Takarazuka Line), take the Hankyu Bus to Myōken-guchi (4 km hike). * From {{ill|Kameoka Station|ja|亀岡駅}} (JR Sagano Line), take the Keihan Kyoto Kotsu bus to Jinchi (5.4 km hike). * From {{ill|Myōken-guchi Station|ja|妙見口駅}} (Nose Electric Railway), hike 3.6 km via the Shintaki Trail. Five hiking routes are available.{{Cite web |url=https://noseden.hankyu.co.jp/guide/hiking/course.html |title=Hiking Routes for Mount Myoken |access-date=2023-11-29 |publisher=Nose Electric Railway }} * Cable cars and chairlifts ceased operation on December 3, 2023.{{cite pressrelease |url=https://noseden.hankyu.co.jp/upload_file/noseden/information/newsrelease202309221.pdf |title=End of Myoken Cable and Related Services |format=PDF |publisher=Nose Electric Railway |date=2023-09-22 |access-date=2023-11-01}}. == References == == See Also == * {{ill|List of mountains in Japan|ja|日本の山一覧}} * {{Redirect|Nose Myokenzan|sacred site near the summit|Nose Myokenzan (Nichiren Buddhism)|temple midway up|Hontaki-ji}} {{Infobox mountain | Name = Mount Myoken | image = [[File:Mount Myoken (Nose)1.jpg|240px]] | Elevation_m = 660.1 | Range = [[Hokusetsu Mountain Range]] | Coordinates = {{Coord|34|55|44.328|N|135|27|59.245|E|display=inline}} | Location = {{ill|Kawanishi, Hyōgo|ja|兵庫県川西市}},
{{ill|Toyono District, Osaka|ja|大阪府豊能郡}} (Toyono Town and Nose Town) | Map = Japan Osaka Prefecture#Japan Hyogo Prefecture | Relief = 1 | Map_caption = Location of Mount Myoken in Osaka and Hyōgo | OSM_zoom = 10 }} '''Mount Myoken''' (妙見山) is a mountain straddling {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}} and {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, part of the [[Hokusetsu Mountain Range]]. == Overview == The mountain itself is a subject of worship. Near the summit is ''{{ill|Nose Myokenzan (Nichiren Buddhism)|ja|能勢妙見山 (Nichiren宗)}}'', an important {{ill|Nichiren Buddhism|ja|Nichiren宗}} temple in the Kansai region. This temple, officially named Murosan Shinnyo-ji Keigai Butsudō Nose Myokenzan, is an enclave of {{ill|Shinnyo-ji (Osaka Prefecture)|ja|真如寺}} in {{ill|Nose, Osaka|ja|能勢町}}, and attracts far more visitors than its parent temple. A fourth-class {{ill|Triangulation station|ja|三角点}} is located at the summit, within Toyono Town, Osaka. The triangulation marker is discreetly placed behind a memorial stone.{{Cite web|url=https://www.google.com/maps/@34.9288684,135.4673572,3a,90y,273.91h,30.3t/data=!3m8!1e1!3m6!1sAF1QipM6B_3w0CwvMR8Wu_YVqLdcIHiS5HpEXgePnAeg!2e10!3e11!6shttps:%2F%2Flh5.googleusercontent.com%2Fp%2FAF1QipM6B_3w0CwvMR8Wu_YVqLdcIHiS5HpEXgePnAeg%3Dw203-h100-k-no-pi-0-ya76.70306-ro-0-fo100!7i8704!8i4352?hl=ja |title=Google Street View: Mount Myoken Triangulation Point |access-date=2017-12-17 |date=2016-05 |publisher=[[Google Street View]] }}. Mount Myoken marks the southernmost natural habitat for {{ill|Japanese beech|ja|ブナ}} at altitudes of around 600 meters in Southwest Japan. Previously thought confined to Osaka Prefecture, eight beech trees have been identified in {{ill|Kawanishi, Hyōgo|ja|川西市}}, and a portion of the 9.5-hectare beech forest near the summit has been confirmed to extend into Hyōgo.http://www.pref.hyogo.lg.jp/JPN/apr/hyogoshizen/reddata2010/rdb2010/data/tan-itsu/tan-itsu_H25_10.pdfhttp://www.kobe-np.co.jp/news/hanshin/201503/0007829215.shtmlhttp://bunamori.org/ The beech habitats in Mount Myoken and {{ill|Izumikatsuragi Mountain|ja|和泉葛城山}} are designated as Natural Monuments. == History == === Summit === The summit was once home to Foraku-san Daikū-ji, reportedly founded by {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基}}. During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the {{ill|Nose clan|ja|能勢氏}}, descended from {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsunaka|ja|源満仲}}, began venerating {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}. In the early {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, Nissei, the 21st abbot of {{ill|Kuon-ji|ja|久遠寺}}, carved a new image of Myōken under the patronage of Lord Nose Yoritsugu and enshrined it in a temple built at Daikū-ji's former site, now known as Nose Myokenzan. The summit’s beech forest, over 10,000 years old, suggests that the site was a place of worship long before Daikū-ji's establishment. === Middle Slopes and Waterfalls === On the northern slope lies {{ill|Hontaki|ja|本滝}}, historically a site for ascetic practices since the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}. The falls are described in the {{ill|Settsu Meisho Zue|ja|摂津名所図会}} as “Myoken Falls,” frequented by Nose Myokenzan worshippers. Adjacent to the falls was Jōtomi-dō, later rebuilt as {{ill|Hontaki-ji|ja|本瀧寺}} in 1935 by Nichiren priest Noma Nisshō, becoming the head temple of {{ill|Myoken sect|ja|妙見宗}}. Many stone statues along the path to the falls reflect its past as a religious training ground. Other waterfalls include {{ill|Shintaki|ja|新滝}} (southwest slope) near {{ill|Shirataki Shrine|ja|白瀧神社}} and {{ill|Seiryū Falls|ja|清滝}} (east slope) near Myōryū-ji Temple. == Access == For detailed information on access to the summit (Nose Myokenzan Main Hall), see {{ill|Nose Myokenzan (Nichiren Buddhism)|ja|能勢妙見山 (Nichiren宗)}}. * From {{ill|Ikeda Station (Osaka)|ja|池田駅}} (Hankyu Takarazuka Line), take the Hankyu Bus to Myōken-guchi (4 km hike). * From {{ill|Kameoka Station|ja|亀岡駅}} (JR Sagano Line), take the Keihan Kyoto Kotsu bus to Jinchi (5.4 km hike). * From {{ill|Myōken-guchi Station|ja|妙見口駅}} (Nose Electric Railway), hike 3.6 km via the Shintaki Trail. Five hiking routes are available.{{Cite web |url=https://noseden.hankyu.co.jp/guide/hiking/course.html |title=Hiking Routes for Mount Myoken |access-date=2023-11-29 |publisher=Nose Electric Railway }} * Cable cars and chairlifts ceased operation on December 3, 2023.{{cite pressrelease |url=https://noseden.hankyu.co.jp/upload_file/noseden/information/newsrelease202309221.pdf |title=End of Myoken Cable and Related Services |format=PDF |publisher=Nose Electric Railway |date=2023-09-22 |access-date=2023-11-01}}. == References == == See Also == * {{ill|List of mountains in Japan|ja|日本の山一覧}} * {{ill|Furusato Hyōgo 50 Mountains|ja|ふるさと兵庫50山}} == External Links == * [https://bunamori.org/ Nose Myokenzan Beech Conservation Society] * [http://www.myoken.org/ Official Website of Nose Myokenzan] During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the {{ill|Nose clan|ja|能勢氏}}, descended from {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsunaka|ja|源満仲}}, began venerating {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}. In the early {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, Nissei, the 21st abbot of {{ill|Kuon-ji|ja|久遠寺}}, carved a new image of Myōken under the patronage of Lord Nose Yoritsugu and enshrined it in a temple built at Daikū-ji's former site, now known as Nose Myokenzan. The summit’s beech forest, over 10,000 years old, suggests that the site was a place of worship long before Daikū-ji's establishment. === Middle Slopes and Waterfalls === On the northern slope lies {{ill|Hontaki|ja|本滝}}, historically a site for ascetic practices since the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}. The falls are described in the {{ill|Settsu Meisho Zue|ja|摂津名所図会}} as “Myoken Falls,” frequented by Nose Myokenzan worshippers. Adjacent to the falls was Jōtomi-dō, later rebuilt as {{ill|Hontaki-ji|ja|本瀧寺}} in 1935 by Nichiren priest Noma Nisshō, becoming the head temple of {{ill|Myoken sect|ja|妙見宗}}. Many stone statues along the path to the falls reflect its past as a religious training ground. Other waterfalls include {{ill|Shintaki|ja|新滝}} (southwest slope) near {{ill|Shirataki Shrine|ja|白瀧神社}} and {{ill|Seiryū Falls|ja|清滝}} (east slope) near Myōryū-ji Temple. == Access == For detailed information on access to the summit (Nose Myokenzan Main Hall), see {{ill|Nose Myokenzan (Nichiren Buddhism)|ja|能勢妙見山 (Nichiren宗)}}. * From {{ill|Ikeda Station (Osaka)|ja|池田駅}} (Hankyu Takarazuka Line), take the Hankyu Bus to Myōken-guchi (4 km hike). * From {{ill|Kameoka Station|ja|亀岡駅}} (JR Sagano Line), take the Keihan Kyoto Kotsu bus to Jinchi (5.4 km hike). * From {{ill|Myōken-guchi Station|ja|妙見口駅}} (Nose Electric Railway), hike 3.6 km via the Shintaki Trail. Five hiking routes are available.{{Cite web |url=https://noseden.hankyu.co.jp/guide/hiking/course.html |title=Hiking Routes for Mount Myoken |access-date=2023-11-29 |publisher=Nose Electric Railway }} * Cable cars and chairlifts ceased operation on December 3, 2023.{{cite pressrelease |url=https://noseden.hankyu.co.jp/upload_file/noseden/information/newsrelease202309221.pdf |title=End of Myoken Cable and Related Services |format=PDF |publisher=Nose Electric Railway |date=2023-09-22 |access-date=2023-11-01}}. == References == == See Also == * {{ill|List of mountains in Japan|ja|日本の山一覧}} * {{Redirect|Nose Myokenzan|sacred site near the summit|Nose Myokenzan (Nichiren Buddhism)|temple midway up|Hontaki-ji}} {{Infobox mountain | Name = Mount Myoken | image = [[File:Mount Myoken (Nose)1.jpg|240px]] | Elevation_m = 660.1 | Range = [[Hokusetsu Mountain Range]] | Coordinates = {{Coord|34|55|44.328|N|135|27|59.245|E|display=inline}} | Location = {{ill|Kawanishi, Hyōgo|ja|兵庫県川西市}},
{{ill|Toyono District, Osaka|ja|大阪府豊能郡}} (Toyono Town and Nose Town) | Map = Japan Osaka Prefecture#Japan Hyogo Prefecture | Relief = 1 | Map_caption = Location of Mount Myoken in Osaka and Hyōgo | OSM_zoom = 10 }} '''Mount Myoken''' (妙見山) is a mountain straddling {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}} and {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, part of the [[Hokusetsu Mountain Range]]. == Overview == The mountain itself is a subject of worship. Near the summit is ''{{ill|Nose Myokenzan (Nichiren Buddhism)|ja|能勢妙見山 (Nichiren宗)}}'', an important {{ill|Nichiren Buddhism|ja|Nichiren宗}} temple in the Kansai region. This temple, officially named Murosan Shinnyo-ji Keigai Butsudō Nose Myokenzan, is an enclave of {{ill|Shinnyo-ji (Osaka Prefecture)|ja|真如寺}} in {{ill|Nose, Osaka|ja|能勢町}}, and attracts far more visitors than its parent temple. A fourth-class {{ill|Triangulation station|ja|三角点}} is located at the summit, within Toyono Town, Osaka. The triangulation marker is discreetly placed behind a memorial stone.{{Cite web|url=https://www.google.com/maps/@34.9288684,135.4673572,3a,90y,273.91h,30.3t/data=!3m8!1e1!3m6!1sAF1QipM6B_3w0CwvMR8Wu_YVqLdcIHiS5HpEXgePnAeg!2e10!3e11!6shttps:%2F%2Flh5.googleusercontent.com%2Fp%2FAF1QipM6B_3w0CwvMR8Wu_YVqLdcIHiS5HpEXgePnAeg%3Dw203-h100-k-no-pi-0-ya76.70306-ro-0-fo100!7i8704!8i4352?hl=ja |title=Google Street View: Mount Myoken Triangulation Point |access-date=2017-12-17 |date=2016-05 |publisher=[[Google Street View]] }}. Mount Myoken marks the southernmost natural habitat for {{ill|Japanese beech|ja|ブナ}} at altitudes of around 600 meters in Southwest Japan. Previously thought confined to Osaka Prefecture, eight beech trees have been identified in {{ill|Kawanishi, Hyōgo|ja|川西市}}, and a portion of the 9.5-hectare beech forest near the summit has been confirmed to extend into Hyōgo.http://www.pref.hyogo.lg.jp/JPN/apr/hyogoshizen/reddata2010/rdb2010/data/tan-itsu/tan-itsu_H25_10.pdfhttp://www.kobe-np.co.jp/news/hanshin/201503/0007829215.shtmlhttp://bunamori.org/ The beech habitats in Mount Myoken and {{ill|Izumikatsuragi Mountain|ja|和泉葛城山}} are designated as Natural Monuments. == History == === Summit === The summit was once home to Foraku-san Daikū-ji, reportedly founded by {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基}}. During the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the {{ill|Nose clan|ja|能勢氏}}, descended from {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsunaka|ja|源満仲}}, began venerating {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}. In the early {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, Nissei, the 21st abbot of {{ill|Kuon-ji|ja|久遠寺}}, carved a new image of Myōken under the patronage of Lord Nose Yoritsugu and enshrined it in a temple built at Daikū-ji's former site, now known as Nose Myokenzan. The summit’s beech forest, over 10,000 years old, suggests that the site was a place of worship long before Daikū-ji's establishment. === Middle Slopes and Waterfalls === On the northern slope lies {{ill|Hontaki|ja|本滝}}, historically a site for ascetic practices since the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}. The falls are described in the {{ill|Settsu Meisho Zue|ja|摂津名所図会}} as “Myoken Falls,” frequented by Nose Myokenzan worshippers. Adjacent to the falls was Jōtomi-dō, later rebuilt as {{ill|Hontaki-ji|ja|本瀧寺}} in 1935 by Nichiren priest Noma Nisshō, becoming the head temple of {{ill|Myoken sect|ja|妙見宗}}. Many stone statues along the path to the falls reflect its past as a religious training ground. Other waterfalls include {{ill|Shintaki|ja|新滝}} (southwest slope) near {{ill|Shirataki Shrine|ja|白瀧神社}} and {{ill|Seiryū Falls|ja|清滝}} (east slope) near Myōryū-ji Temple. == Access == For detailed information on access to the summit (Nose Myokenzan Main Hall), see {{ill|Nose Myokenzan (Nichiren Buddhism)|ja|能勢妙見山 (Nichiren宗)}}. * From {{ill|Ikeda Station (Osaka)|ja|池田駅}} (Hankyu Takarazuka Line), take the Hankyu Bus to Myōken-guchi (4 km hike). * From {{ill|Kameoka Station|ja|亀岡駅}} (JR Sagano Line), take the Keihan Kyoto Kotsu bus to Jinchi (5.4 km hike). * From {{ill|Myōken-guchi Station|ja|妙見口駅}} (Nose Electric Railway), hike 3.6 km via the Shintaki Trail. Five hiking routes are available.{{Cite web |url=https://noseden.hankyu.co.jp/guide/hiking/course.html |title=Hiking Routes for Mount Myoken |access-date=2023-11-29 |publisher=Nose Electric Railway }} * Cable cars and chairlifts ceased operation on December 3, 2023.{{cite pressrelease |url=https://noseden.hankyu.co.jp/upload_file/noseden/information/newsrelease202309221.pdf |title=End of Myoken Cable and Related Services |format=PDF |publisher=Nose Electric Railway |date=2023-09-22 |access-date=2023-11-01}}. == References == == See Also == * {{ill|List of mountains in Japan|ja|日本の山一覧}} *
